title: Health Insurance — How to Choose the Right Plan
meta_description: Complete guide to health insurance in India. How much cover you need, what to check, cashless vs reimbursement, and top mistakes to avoid.

hero.one_liner: One hospital visit can wipe out years of savings — health insurance is not optional, it's essential. Here's how to choose right.

sections.heading: Why You Need Health Insurance
content: A single hospitalization in a private hospital costs ₹2-5 lakh on average. A surgery can cost ₹5-15 lakh. ICU charges run ₹10,000-50,000 per day. Without insurance, one medical emergency can drain your savings, force you to break investments, or push you into debt.

Your employer's group health insurance is not enough — it covers ₹3-5 lakh typically, disappears when you change jobs or retire, and may not cover your parents. You need a personal health insurance policy that stays with you for life.

sections.heading: How Much Cover Do You Need?

rows.label: Married, no kids
rows.value: ₹10 lakh family floater + ₹20 lakh super top-up

rows.label: Family with kids
rows.value: ₹15-20 lakh family floater + ₹25 lakh super top-up

rows.label: Parents (50+)
rows.value: ₹10-15 lakh separate policy + super top-up. Buy ASAP — premiums rise sharply with age.

steps.title: Claim settlement ratio
steps.description: Check the insurer's CSR — what percentage of claims they actually pay. Aim for 95%+ CSR. Check on irdai.gov.in annual reports. Low CSR means they reject too many claims.

steps.title: Network hospitals in your city
steps.description: Cashless treatment only works at network hospitals. Check if your preferred hospitals (near home and work) are on the insurer's list. More hospitals = better.

steps.title: No room rent sub-limits
steps.description: Avoid policies that cap room rent at ₹3,000-5,000/day. If your room costs ₹8,000 but policy covers ₹5,000, ALL other expenses (surgeon fees, medicines) are proportionally reduced. Choose plans with no room rent cap.

steps.title: Pre and post hospitalization
steps.description: Good policies cover 30-60 days pre-hospitalization (tests, consultations) and 60-180 days post-hospitalization (medicines, follow-ups). More days = better coverage.

steps.title: Restoration benefit
steps.description: If you use your full sum insured in one claim, restoration benefit refills it for subsequent claims in the same year. Essential for family floater plans where one major illness can exhaust the cover.

sections.heading: Cashless vs Reimbursement
content: Cashless means the hospital bills the insurance company directly — you don't pay anything upfront (except non-covered items). This only works at network hospitals. You need to show your health card and the insurer pre-approves the treatment.

Reimbursement means you pay the hospital first, then submit bills to the insurer and get money back. This takes 15-30 days. You can use this at any hospital, including non-network ones. Always prefer cashless when possible.

faqs.question: Best age to buy health insurance?
faqs.answer: As early as possible — ideally in your 20s. Premiums are cheapest when you're young and healthy. A ₹10 lakh policy at age 25 costs ₹5,000-8,000/year. The same policy at 45 costs ₹15,000-25,000. Plus, diseases that develop later may become 'pre-existing conditions' and face waiting periods.

faqs.question: Does health insurance cover pre-existing diseases?
faqs.answer: Yes, but after a waiting period of typically 2-4 years. Diseases you had before buying the policy (diabetes, BP, thyroid, etc.) are covered only after this waiting period. Some policies offer reduced waiting periods of 1-2 years at higher premiums.

faqs.question: Can I claim tax benefit on health insurance?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under Section 80D: up to ₹25,000 for self/family premium (₹50,000 if anyone is 60+), plus up to ₹25,000 for parents' premium (₹50,000 if parents are 60+). Maximum total deduction: ₹1 lakh if both you and parents are senior citizens.

faqs.question: Family floater vs individual plans?
faqs.answer: Family floater covers the entire family under one sum insured — cheaper but if one person makes a big claim, less remains for others. Individual plans give separate cover to each person — more expensive but safer. Best combo: family floater for daily family + super top-up for catastrophic coverage.

faqs.question: Is Ayushman Bharat enough?
faqs.answer: Ayushman Bharat (PMJAY) covers ₹5 lakh for eligible BPL families — it's great but only for hospitalization at empanelled hospitals with specific treatments. It doesn't cover OPD, diagnostics outside hospitalization, or non-empanelled hospitals. If you can afford it, get a private policy too.

disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only. Insurance plans vary widely. Compare policies on the insurer's website and read the policy document before purchasing. Consult an insurance advisor for personalized recommendations.
